title: Dog Slow to Rise From Sitting: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog struggling to stand? Learn about common causes, why the urgency is usually low, and how photos help your vet diagnose mobility issues.
content_html:
TL;DR: Difficulty rising is usually a sign of chronic joint stiffness or arthritis, requiring a non-emergency veterinary consultation. Seek more urgent care if your dog is yelping, refusing to eat, or suddenly unable to use their legs.

What does it mean if my dog is having trouble getting up or is slow to rise?

  • Struggling to rise from a sitting or lying position usually indicates joint stiffness, muscle weakness, or underlying discomfort.
  • This is most commonly associated with Osteoarthritis, a degenerative condition where joint cartilage wears down.
  • Other possibilities include hip or elbow dysplasia or simple muscle strain.
  • The behavior is a sign that your dog's body is compensating for physical changes or pain.

How urgent is it if my dog is slow to rise and when should I see a vet?

  • In most cases, being slow to rise is a chronic, slow-developing issue rather than a sudden medical emergency.
  • Schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to discuss a long-term management plan.
  • Contact a clinic sooner if your dog is also yelping in pain or refuses to eat.
  • Seek immediate care if your dog is suddenly unable to use one or more legs at all.

Why is it helpful to take a video of my dog's movement for the veterinarian?

  • Capturing a video of your dog's movement is one of the most helpful tools for a veterinary professional.
  • Dogs often experience an adrenaline rush at the clinic, which may temporarily hide their stiffness or limp.
  • A video of them rising in their natural home environment allows the vet to see the exact nature of the struggle.
  • Visual evidence helps the vet distinguish between orthopedic issues (like arthritis) and neurological issues (like nerve weakness).

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In older dogs, a reluctance or slowness to rise from a sitting position can be indicative of several underlying conditions, including cognitive dysfunction syndrome, intervertebral disc disease (particularly in the cervical and thoracolumbar regions), lumbosacral disease, pain, sensory decline, or diseases affecting the central nervous system or circulation. Owners should report these signs when they arise. Clinical signs of lumbosacral disease typically begin at 3-7 years of age and may include difficulty using the pelvic limbs, pelvic limb lameness, tail weakness, and incontinence. A thorough veterinary examination, including a detailed history, physical and neurologic evaluation, and appropriate diagnostic tests (radiographs, MRI, CT, or epidurography), is essential to determine the underlying cause and initiate appropriate management.

Chapter: Neurology, Geriatrics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1569)

faqs.question: Is my dog just getting old?
faqs.answer: While stiffness is common in senior dogs, 'old age' itself isn't a diagnosis. Most older dogs who are slow to rise are suffering from manageable arthritic pain that can be significantly improved with the right treatment.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give human medications like Ibuprofen or Naproxen to dogs, as they are highly toxic to canines. Your vet can prescribe safe, pet-specific anti-inflammatories.

faqs.question: What can I do at home to help?
faqs.answer: Providing supportive orthopedic bedding and placing non-slip rugs over hardwood or tile floors can give your dog the traction and comfort they need to rise more easily.
